data.title: What are the characteristics of projects that have been delisted by major exchanges?
data.description:
Author: Hu Tao, ChainCatcher

In the crypto industry, listing a coin once meant the birth of another wealth-creation myth. Now, it may just be the prelude to a long cleanup process.

On March 18, Binance announced that it would stop trading and delist 8 tokens, including Ampleforth (FORTH), Hooked Protocol (HOOK), IDEX (IDEX), Loopring (LRC), Neutron (NTRN), and Radiant Capital (RDNT).

On March 18, Binance announced that it would stop trading and delist 8 tokens, including Ampleforth (FORTH), Hooked Protocol (HOOK), IDEX (IDEX), Loopring (LRC), Neutron (NTRN), and Radiant Capital (RDNT).

Author: Hu Tao, ChainCatcher ...Author: Hu Tao, ChainCatcher

II. Transparency is Becoming a Red Line

Besides insufficient industry momentum, lack of transparency is also one of the main reasons why many projects have been delisted.

With the increasing regulatory scrutiny of the crypto industry and the growing risk awareness of investors, exchanges are becoming increasingly stringent in their requirements for the transparency of token projects. According to its official website, Binance has explicitly included "the level of public communication, community participation, and transparency of the project team" and "the team's commitment to the project" in its evaluation criteria for token health. This means that clear team and roadmap information, a sound information disclosure mechanism, and active community communication channels are crucial for any token. However, for many projects, the "listing and then lying low" mentality has become an awkward and harsh reality. According to the transparency score recently released by RootData, most of the tokens delisted by exchanges like Binance had transparency scores below 70%, indicating varying degrees of problems such as insufficient disclosure of project progress and missing team members. Stagnant community communication has become the norm, which leads to a significant weakening of user attention and even trading intentions, thus creating a vicious cycle of insufficient trading volume and liquidity. Taking Ultiverse, invested in by YZi Labs, as an example, the project has barely posted any tweets since January, only retweeting a few messages, and the same goes for a few core team members. This "black box" operation not only challenges the risk defenses of exchanges but also directly infringes on the right to know of retail investors. This collective "cleanup" by exchanges is essentially a supply-side reform targeting "bad money," allocating more resources to assets with high transparency and solid competitiveness. In this way, exchanges are creating a systemic deterrent against projects on their exchanges: transparency is no longer a soft "bonus," but a necessity for survival. With the accelerated penetration of traditional capital and the gradual clarification of the global regulatory framework, the competitive landscape among exchanges has undergone a qualitative change. The focus is no longer on trading volume and user numbers, but rather on the quality of underlying assets and the platform's compliance. The synchronized actions of leading exchanges such as Binance, Coinbase, and OKX indicate that a "dehydration" cycle, aimed at squeezing out the bubble, has begun.

data.title: GEO didn't panic after March 15th: Industry practitioners are happy.
data.description:
Author | Pencil News Meng Qia

An unusual phenomenon: After the 3.15 exposé of AI poisoning, the GEO industry is actually doing better.

Industry insiders say: Customers are flocking in, and the opportunities are just beginning.

GEO, is it poison?

Author | Pencil News Meng Qia

A strange phenomenon: After the 3.15 exposé of AI poisoning, the GEO industry is actually doing better.

Is GEO poison?

Author | Pencil News Meng QiaAuthor | Pencil News Meng QiaAuthor | Pencil News Meng Qia

Is GEO poison? Should it be eliminated or guided? GEO, simply put, is about using AI for marketing. This relatively new concept was prematurely introduced to the public eye by CCTV. Interestingly, the initial reaction from those in the industry was not panic. "After the 3.15 report, I was not surprised at all; in fact, I was quite happy," said Jiao Shidou, CEO of Model Power Index. "The official media's quick reaction is a good thing, and it also shows that GEO's influence is expanding. If it is exploited by some black hat techniques, the long-term harm will be significant." Jiao Shidou dares to say this because he is an insider. The company he founded, Model Power Index, is a GEO (Geographical Analysis) firm specializing in large-scale model brand awareness management. The "black hat" tactics he mentioned are precisely the core of the 3.15 exposé—poisoning. Simply put, poisoning is feeding AI a bunch of lies, but packaging them to look like the truth. Its biggest harm is that AI starts to lie seriously. AI no longer gives you a trustworthy world, but a "manipulated world" full of lies. "CCTV has taught us a lesson about its harms," ​​said Xu Xiaohui, founder of Wanwu Tianze Marketing Consulting, expressing his genuine feelings. He is a veteran in the marketing industry, having previously served as VP of Vancl and Cheetah Mobile. "Before the 3.15 exposé, I didn't think it was AI poisoning; my impression wasn't that bad. Before, I thought GEO was just a normal industry, like SEO (Search Engine Optimization) back in the day, with its own online trolls spreading comments," said Xu Xiaohui. Objectively speaking, "poisoning" has never disappeared in the past 10 years; it's just constantly changing its guise. In the PC era, the search engines we used also had a large number of businesses "brushing rankings" and spreading fake content. The forums and online communities we frequented also had a large number of online trolls influencing public opinion, such as saying "Company X is shady" or "Product X is terrible." In the mobile internet era, even on Weibo, which we use, there are still online trolls creating "hot topics" and pushing them to the top of the trending searches. The WeChat official accounts we browse daily are bombarded with KOL-sponsored articles every day, seemingly an "industry consensus." Therefore, no matter how rapidly technology advances, the phenomenon of "poisoning" has never ceased; only the form has changed: from manipulating search rankings and comments to "manipulating AI algorithms." "But I completely understand why CCTV is paying so much attention. Its spread is too fast; it will cause bad money to drive out good, and the speed is terrifying," said Xu Xiaohui. AI-driven poisoning has long been met with united resistance from the industry. Lu Zhiming, product manager of Juyou Universe, told LeadDao that the company has joined the anti-poisoning alliance. Juyou Universe's current business is "AI + Marketing," and they have long been interested in GEO services but have yet to implement them. "The industry is still very unregulated and lacks a systematic approach. We are a little worried about entering the market too early." Against this backdrop of unregulated activity, similar anti-poisoning organizations are already quite numerous. Last November, 14 domestic GEO-related companies launched the "China GEO Industry Development Initiative," proposing to "reject falsehoods and oppose pollution." The day before the 3.15 Gala, 16 GEO companies signed the "Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) Industry Self-Discipline Convention," explicitly resisting behaviors such as AI poisoning. CCTV's 3.15 Gala is a mirror; some saw the dangers and distanced themselves further from GEO; but others saw the opportunities and are embracing GEO. After all, the 3.15 Gala didn't define GEO as a technology, but only addressed the phenomenon of "poisoning" it. "Some companies were initially hesitant, unsure if GEO would be effective, but after watching the 3.15 Gala, they became even more determined. What solidified their resolve wasn't the poisoning itself, but the fact that using large-scale marketing models could genuinely be effective," said Lü Zhiming. GEO is a neutral technology. If we speak with facts, AI will thrive; if we poison it, AI will become synonymous with lies. Whether it goes left or right depends on its environment and conditions. "The day after the 3.15 Gala, we held a weekly meeting. Everyone unanimously agreed that relevant national laws, platform rules, and large-scale model reference standards would all undergo a wave of changes," said Lü Zhiming. Surprisingly, after the 3.15 exposé, GEO concept stocks in the secondary market actually rose. Tianlong Group, Yidian Tianxia, ​​and Chinese Online were heavily traded, for one simple reason: they were believed to have established GEO businesses. However, most of the companies named in the 3.15 exposé launched a "triple denial" campaign. Tianlong Group publicly stated that it did not engage in GEO business as defined by the market, did not directly engage in AI business, and did not generate additional revenue from AI tools. Behind the GEO concept stocks lies a hidden fact: what people see is not the "poisoning" itself, but the new marketing solution brought by GEO. From this perspective, the 3.15 exposé has actually become a reverse endorsement of GEO. Lü Zhiming told Pencil News that in the week following the 3.15 exposé, clients inquired about GEO business. Jiao Shidou also felt the positive changes. After 3.15, the number of clients consulting him increased. Some didn't know about GEO, but now they do; others knew about it but were observing, but now they feel it's time to take action. Before the 3.15 exposé, some of these clients had never even used AI large-scale models, let alone GEO. Behind these demands lies the marketing anxiety that brands can't suppress: after all, the old marketing tools are no longer as effective. The familiar online combination of WeChat, Weibo, Douyin, and search, along with offline advertising like Focus Media's screen-based advertising, is finding increasingly limited room for growth. Taking search engines as an example, traffic continues to decline, a trend unlikely to reverse. After the release of ChatGPT at the end of 2022, Google's global search share fell from 93% to 89.7% in April of this year, and has remained below 90% for the past five months. Meanwhile, in China, Baidu's advertising revenue is also declining. According to the latest financial report, Baidu's annual advertising revenue was approximately 62.2 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 14.7%. Compared to its peak in 2018, it has fallen by about 20 billion yuan. Search habits have changed, and the window for brand exposure has shrunk. In the past, a single search could return hundreds of links, allowing users to compare and filter, providing brands with marketing opportunities. Now, however, more and more scenarios are becoming where AI directly provides a dozen lines of answers, or even a conclusion. Users no longer "select information" but directly accept the results. In this situation, brands easily fall into a kind of "loss of control anxiety": the entry point has changed, the display positions have decreased, and they fear they won't even have the chance to be "seen." This anxiety further fuels the rampant growth of "AI poisoning." The specific methods are not complicated at all, and can even be described as "crude." For example, some service providers will operate hundreds of accounts simultaneously, repeatedly posting the same set of content on different platforms: the "advantages" of the same brand, the "problems" of the same competitor, rewritten in different versions and distributed in Q&A, articles, and communities. These contents, in themselves, may not be obviously fake at first glance, but the problem lies in the fact that they are deliberately manufactured "uniform voices." Once this content becomes large enough and is "consumed" by AI, a change occurs: some content that was originally just marketing rhetoric begins to become "conclusions" in the AI's discourse. How does intelligent AI get poisoned? A real problem is that current GEO technology is not yet adept at handling this kind of "poisoning" tactic. Large-scale models generally use the RAG (Research, Analysis, and Grammar) model: first research, then answer. For example, search 10 to 30 articles, extract key content, and finally generate the answer. When someone "poisons" the AI ​​by consistently stating the same thing, the AI ​​will think, "Many people are saying this," and may accept it, thus becoming "poisoned." Moreover, large-scale models in China are inherently not objective enough. Baseline AI CEO Li Mingxuan observed a phenomenon: Doubao leans towards ByteDance, Yuanbao towards Tencent, Baidu users prefer Baidu accounts, and Kimi and DeepSeek, lacking their own platforms, primarily scrape content from Zhihu, Sohu, and other platforms. "The spread efficiency of this 'poisoning' is terrifying; it's hundreds or even thousands of times faster than the old methods of online trolls," Xu Xiaohui told Pencil News. He explained that in the SEO era, people were proactive. They had to search for and compare prices themselves before buying anything, and wouldn't let one or two articles influence their judgment. Now, people are passive; AI will directly provide the answer. And this answer may directly influence your judgment. Furthermore, the spread of SEO is slow. Writing advertorials with paid online commentators requires hiring people, publishing articles, and maintaining accounts—it's costly and noisy. Now, even small workshops with only 1-2 people use AI to rewrite articles, producing thousands a day at almost zero cost. Despite the significant harm of this "poisoning" behavior, many interviewees unanimously believe it won't last long. "This is a business model that fools idiots," Li Mingxuan bluntly stated. A more direct reason: the poisoning exploited a "vulnerability" in the large model, and these "vulnerabilities" can be patched technically. "For example, Google's EAT framework (professionalism, authority, credibility) and cross-validation can effectively classify information sources," said Li Mingxuan. Jiao Shidou mentioned Gemini's approach: hiding the information source, thus increasing the difficulty of poisoning. When you don't know which materials the AI ​​referenced, it's difficult to specifically "feed" it false information. Lü Zhiming is exploring another approach: cross-validating multiple models and allowing them to compete against each other. "For example, the content viewed on chatgpt will be reviewed by Gemini to see if it's factual." As the "immunity" of the large model increases, more malicious attempts will become ineffective. Jiao Shidou mentioned an interesting phenomenon to Pencil News: After March 15th (Consumer Rights Day), some customers who left due to low prices returned. The reason is not complicated: some low-price service providers charged customers for six months, with the first two months being valid, and the remaining three months being invalid. What's the reason? Because the large models have iterated, and the operational threshold has increased. Jiao Shidou mentioned that the current window of opportunity is very special; domestic large models are still fighting amongst themselves and can't afford to focus on refined management. Once the market structure stabilizes, they will turn around and plug the loopholes—it's only a matter of time. "Even without 3.15, the market will automatically purify itself." GEO World: How to Do It? Although GEO is a new species, all sorts of people are vying for it. Xu Xiaohui divided marketers familiar with this circle into several schools of thought. 1. Self-taught workshops. 1. **One or two people use AI to produce thousands of articles a day, stuffing them into platforms with vulnerabilities.** The cost is extremely low, a few hundred yuan is all it takes to get a job, with a promise of "getting your product on the list in seven days." But the approach is simple and brutal—quantity over quality. 2. **The Tech-Savvy Group.** These are usually teams from large companies, knowledgeable in algorithms, with resources, controlling thousands of accounts, and even some hacking skills. They don't rely on sheer volume; they use precise technical intervention to manipulate large models, making AI "naturally" accept their content. 3. **The Results-Based Group.** "Your ranking determines how much I charge," based on results. After March 15th (Consumer Rights Day), the rules changed. Xu Xiaohui predicts: legitimate players will enter the market. Previously, collaborating with media editors was enough to get articles published, but now people "dare not do that anymore," and the approach has become more systematic. Among these different approaches, who will succeed depends on one key factor: whoever's marketing plan can influence AI will be the one to make money. How to influence AI? The content creation mindset needs to change. Previously, content was "for people to read," now it's shifting to "for AI to read." What kind of content does AI like? Xu Xiaohui has several answers. 1. Structured information. "You need to fully express your identity—who you are, what kind of audience you're targeting, competitor comparisons, and your industry ranking; then collaborate with authoritative media. This way, one article can be worth 100 or even 1000 advertorials." 2. "Human-like" information. "After GPT emerged, the proportion of AI-generated content skyrocketed from 0% to over 50%. Content written by real people has become scarce," said Xu Xiaohui. This is a huge paradox for those working on GEO. They used AI to mass-produce garbage, but the result was a surge in the value of content with a "human touch." Now, large model platforms have learned their lesson—they can no longer feed AI content; it will only make it dumber. Li Mingxuan also believes that companies need to change their marketing strategies. Instead of cramming in ads, they should write more "worth-learning" content. "Don't cramm in ads; write more content that benefits others: how the product is designed, what pitfalls were encountered, how the brand was built. When AI references this content, it will find it valuable." Of course, the evolution of GEO will still take time. On the one hand, large-scale model vulnerabilities won't disappear overnight, and the practice of exploiting AI will still have fertile ground to thrive; the offensive and defensive battles will continue. On the other hand, at least one thing is certain: the barrier to entry for manipulating AI under its nose will only increase in the future.

data.title: Looking at the history of the Web through OpenClaw: When AI has sovereignty, what will humanity have left?
data.description:
Recently, the term "Web4" has become very popular, especially after the emergence of multi-agent (AI agent) frameworks like OpenClaw, which have led to more frequent mentions of AI and crypto. Over the past two weeks, I've discussed and shared with friends at various online and offline events, and I want to say to those interested in Web4: forget the obscure code and technical jargon, and let's return to the essence of Web4. From Web1 to Web4, this has never been a simple technological iteration. It's a history of power evolution concerning who owns the data, how wealth is distributed, and who controls productivity. Understanding the transfer of power is key to understanding where future money and opportunities will flow. Web1: The Era of One-Way Broadcast of Power and Read-Only Representation The early internet was like a giant library moved to a screen. Sina, Sohu, and Yahoo were the kings of that era. The defining characteristic of this era was: Power structure: One-dimensional. The platforms held the microphone. They wrote, we read. They decided what the headlines were today, and that's all we could discuss. Asset ownership: None of your business. At this stage, users had no digital assets. We were merely traffic, pairs of eyes in front of the screen. In short, compared to the pre-internet era, Web 1 broke down physical distances, allowing information to spread at zero cost. But it had a fatal flaw: Ordinary people couldn't participate in value creation, let alone share in the profits. Thus, the era moved forward. Web2: Panopticon and the Invisible Disposal of Assets This is the era we live in today. WeChat, Douyin, Didi. We not only consume content, we create it. We post on WeChat Moments, hail rides, and order takeout. On the surface, this era appears to have decentralized power, with everyone having an account. But in reality, it represents the largest-scale invisible dispossession of assets in human history. The characteristics of this era— The form of power: Panopticon dictatorship. Borrowing from philosopher Foucault, a super platform is a "panopticon prison." Algorithms watch you from the central tower, recording every click you make. Platforms are both rule-makers and referees. A single agreement can permanently destroy your social media life. Asset Ownership: A Complete Misalignment of Labor and Income. You contribute all the data, feed the algorithm, but the trillions in market value generated by this data belongs to the platform's shareholders, not you. You only have the "right to use" your account, followers, and game items, not "ownership." This kind of gameplay inevitably leads to monopolies and will inevitably backfire. Antitrust fines are increasing, and user dissatisfaction is piling up. The business world needs a "violent dismantling"—returning what belongs to everyone to everyone. Web3: Your Things Are Truly Yours. In my eyes, Web3 is not a cryptocurrency game at all. It's a digital power movement—every ordinary person reclaiming what rightfully belongs to them from internet giants. Its core weapon is cryptography. It doesn't believe the giants' "We don't do evil," it only believes in the mathematical principle of "You can't do evil." This era looks like—no more trust in banks, no more trust in large corporations. Trust is entrusted to distributed nodes and open-source code. The form of companies is changing; DAOs have emerged—organizations without bosses, where everyone votes and makes decisions.

  • Assets: What's yours is yours, and no one can take it away

For the first time in human history, you can truly "own" a digital asset without any institution endorsing it. As long as you possess the private key (a password only you understand), no platform can freeze your wallet. The rules are no longer dictated by the platform, but hard-coded and unchangeable by anyone.

But reality isn't so rosy.

In the disputes we've handled, we've seen countless instances of "code is law" clashing head-on with real-world laws—hacking cryptocurrency theft, transnational money laundering, contract vulnerabilities. Web3 is still wild, full of pitfalls.

But it must be admitted that Web3 has indeed built a financial settlement system that traditional rules can't handle.

It's ready in every way, except for one thing—a tireless "labor force" to truly put it to use. Now, the singularity has arrived. Web3, the sword in the stone, has finally found its master—AI. The EU has given Web4 a grand definition, calling it a fusion of AI, IoT, blockchain, and XR. But breaking it down, the core business logic is simple: Web4 = AI Agent (AI that can do the work) + Crypto (money that machines can use) Large models are merely tools for conversation, but AI Agents are different—they can work on their own, trade on their own, and make money on their own.

1. Why must AI use Crypto for payments?

Imagine: Your AI assistant discovers an investment opportunity and needs to buy data from another company's AI. The problem is—how do these two programs transact?

Banks won't open an account with a single line of code. Alipay doesn't support a thousand transactions per second between two AIs, each costing only a few cents. Only Crypto can handle this.

Crypto is essentially "money for machines." In Web4, AI will have its own wallet; it works for itself, spends its own money, and signs its own contracts. While you sleep, your AI may have already worked all night and earned money for you.

2. Power: Humans Are Starting to Lose Control In Web4, power has "overflowed" from human hands for the first time. AI is no longer a tool, but an independent "economic agent." You can hire a team of AIs, which will automatically divide tasks, negotiate prices, and even negotiate cooperation among themselves. You only need to give instructions, and they will handle the rest. Humans are starting to change from "doers" to "instructors." 3. Trouble: Who Takes the Blame When AI Causes Mishap? This is a real problem we are facing. If an AI with tens of millions in assets suddenly "goes crazy," manipulates the market, or signs a contract that will bankrupt you—who will be responsible?

Catch the programmers who write the code? Sue the big model companies? Or you, the "master"?

Traditional corporate law and contract law are all ineffective here. Before the machine economy explodes, the legal loopholes must be filled first.

4. The Future: Heaven or Abyss?

The end point of Web4 may be two completely opposite directions——

  • Ideal state: Complete liberation of productivity. AI takes care of all the hard and tiring work, Crypto eliminates middlemen and profits from the difference. Humans will finally not have to worry about making a living, and can concentrate on creation and decision-making, no longer being cogs in the machine.

    Humans will finally not have to worry about making a living, and can concentrate on creation and decision-making, no longer being cogs in the machine.

Conclusion

The wheels of history are crushing old consensuses. Power is shifting to algorithms, and assets are shifting to on-chain. Standing at the threshold of Web4, fear is meaningless, and blind obedience is disastrous. Understand the underlying logic and find the legitimacy of innovation on the edge of the rules. We look forward to walking side by side with reliable partners in the future world.

Original Authors: Zhao Xuan, Mao Jiehao

data.title: What are Ethereum and Vitalik trying to do?
data.description:
In many people's traditional understanding, Ethereum's core positioning has always been that of a "world computer" or a "global settlement layer." For the past decade, it has indeed been responsible for executing smart contracts, hosting DeFi, and supporting NFTs, essentially becoming a programmable financial and application execution layer. However, on March 12th, Vitalik Buterin proposed a rather refreshing perspective—the crypto industry may have overcomplicated the practical uses of blockchain. Ethereum's most fundamental value may not be the smart contract function we've been emphasizing, but rather a very simple primitive: a globally shared "public bulletin board" in a cryptographic sense. Many users may wonder, is this shift from "computer" to "bulletin board" a functional regression, or is there another consideration at play?

I. The "Global Shared Memory" Behind "Bulletin Boards"

The term "public bulletin board," as the name suggests, refers to data availability.

It's easy to understand. We can imagine a giant bulletin board posted in a city center square, readable by anyone, irrevocable, and uncensored. Here, we're referring to a global bulletin board: Global users can confirm the data's existence; even the most powerful government cannot erase it, and no administrator can prevent you from posting compliant content.

Ultimately, the core need of many current digital systems, such as secure online voting and software version control, is not complex financial transactions, but rather a censorship-resistant and publicly verifiable data publishing space—precisely the "bulletin board" that cryptography has long sought: Secure Voting Systems. Traditional electronic voting relies on centralized databases, which are vulnerable to tampering. Publishing voting records on Ethereum allows anyone to verify the results, while the privacy of the ballots is protected by cryptography; Certificate Revocation Systems. The revocation lists for HTTPS certificates and software signing certificates require a publicly verifiable and tamper-proof data source. Blockchain is naturally suited for this role; Multi-Party Coordination and Governance. Open-source projects, decentralized governance, and community funds—these scenarios require collaboration among multiple parties without mutual trust. Ethereum can act as a neutral coordinating layer to publish data and verify actions. These scenarios share a common characteristic: they don't require Ethereum to "run" anything; they only require Ethereum to "remember" something. Vitalik therefore provides a more precise ultimate definition: Ethereum is global shared memory. Anyone can write to it, anyone can read it, and no one can unilaterally erase it—not a company, not a government, and not even Vitalik himself. This positioning also corresponds to a clear technological path. EIP-4844 (Blob data) in 2024 was the first expansion of this bulletin board, and the full implementation of PeerDAS (Peer Data Availability Sampling) in 2026 will increase the "area" of the bulletin board a hundredfold. Ethereum is no longer fixated on the TPS of its main chain, but instead is committed to becoming the world's largest and most secure data storage center, a foundational layer providing globally shared data availability. II. AI Arrives, Public Bulletin Boards Become More Necessary Understanding the essence of "bulletin boards" reveals that the arrival of AI is not two separate things, but rather two sides of the same coin. Objectively speaking, the idea behind "bulletin boards" is closely related to the impact of AI on Web3. This is because more and more people are interacting with AI more often than with any other person each day, and current AI services link your questions—what you ask, when you ask them, and how many times—to your real identity. For example, using ChatGPT requires an email address and credit card; calling the Claude API provides clear billing records—every prompt is a digital trace pointing to you. Therefore, in February 2026, Vitalik and Davide Crapis, head of AI at the Ethereum Foundation, jointly released a proposal called ZK API Usage Credits, aiming to achieve anonymous calls to large AI models using zero-knowledge proofs. The logic of the solution is clear: A user deposits funds (e.g., 100 USDC) into a smart contract, and the contract records this deposit in an on-chain encrypted list. Afterward, each time the AI ​​API is called, the user does not need to expose their identity; they only need to generate a zero-knowledge proof proving "I have the right to use this credit limit" to complete the call. What does this solution need? A public bulletin board, a publicly verifiable and tamper-proof data layer, used to record "who has how much credit limit," not "who is who." At the same time, the proliferation of AI agents brings another new problem: how can these automatically running programs achieve economic cooperation among themselves? After all, when an AI agent needs to call upon the services of another AI agent, it needs to make payments, build reputation, and resolve disputes, but it lacks a bank account, legal identity, and "real-name information" that can be trusted by centralized platforms. Ethereum, as the economic coordination layer for AI agents, provides a natural answer. Agents can initiate transactions, stake collateral, and build verifiable reputation records on-chain—all built upon the transparent data layer provided by that "bulletin board." Within a larger framework, this relationship between Ethereum and AI is even integrated—as AI becomes more powerful, the need for privacy protection, verifiability, and decentralization becomes increasingly rigid. Therefore, Ethereum is not competing with AI, but rather aiming to become the most needed infrastructure in the AI ​​era: a public data layer that anyone can write to, anyone can trust, and no one can shut down.

III. Is the narrative of "smart contracts" insufficient?

Perhaps in Vitalik Buterin's vision, future Ethereum users may not primarily be "humans," but rather AI agents.

Therefore, this shift in positioning from "world computer" to "bulletin board," while easily misinterpreted as lowering expectations, is actually the opposite.

"world computer" is a narrative from an internal perspective, asking "what can our technology do?" while "bulletin board" is a perspective from an external demand perspective, asking "what does the world truly need?"

This may also be attributed to the group of people Vitalik met at cryptography conferences—those researchers of voting systems, designers of certificate protocols, and developers of privacy tools—who had no interest in blockchain and Ethereum, but whose needs were precisely what Ethereum could provide.

Therefore, I believe Ethereum is indeed becoming more realistic step by step, because this is the proper attitude of a mature technology: no longer trying to define application scenarios, but instead refining itself into a sufficiently reliable infrastructure, waiting for the scenarios that truly need it to naturally emerge. Just as TCP/IP doesn't explain what the internet can do, but without TCP/IP, the internet can't do anything. From this perspective, this is perhaps Ethereum's way of "reflecting on its own shortcomings when things don't go its way." After all, the most core and irreplaceable value of blockchain is always that truth that is independent of anyone's will. That means that no matter how fast AI evolves, no matter how blurred the line between reality and illusion becomes, as long as this bulletin board exists, humanity will have a place to store "truth." This, perhaps, is Ethereum's most honest self-positioning.

data.title: A complete ban on overseas exchanges? A comprehensive look at Vietnam's new cryptocurrency regulatory strategy.
data.description: As the global crypto landscape enters a new phase of regulatory restructuring, a crucial battle for control of the crypto "border" has quietly begun in Southeast Asia. On March 17, 2026, a Reuters report revealed a Vietnamese Ministry of Finance document showing that the country was planning to ban its citizens from using overseas cryptocurrency exchanges such as Binance and OKX, and was pushing forward with the pilot launch of the first batch of domestically licensed digital asset exchanges. This move means that this active market, ranking fourth globally in crypto adoption and with an annual trading volume exceeding $200 billion, is about to transform from a global free port into a tightly regulated crypto fortress. The Logic of Regulation's Shift Vietnam's ambivalent attitude towards crypto assets stems from the sheer size of its market and the risk of it spiraling out of control. Chainalysis data shows that in the 12 months ending June 2025, Vietnam's digital asset transaction volume exceeded $200 billion, ranking fourth globally in adoption index. Crypto applications have been deeply integrated into remittances, savings, and even gaming. However, almost all of this massive $200 billion in transactions occurred on overseas platforms. Capital flows, data flows, and potential transaction fees and taxes all flowed overseas. Against the backdrop of Vietnam's strict foreign exchange controls and a lack of high-quality domestic investment channels (stock market confidence crisis, high real estate volatility, and long-term gold premium), cryptocurrency has become one of the main channels for capital outflow. Phan Duc Trung, Chairman of the Vietnam Blockchain Association, stated the core demand: "Establishing local exchanges will help keep transaction fees within the country and support the growth of the digital economy." Therefore, the core logic of the new policy is not to suppress crypto, but to replace the unregulated trading market with compliant exchanges, bringing the out-of-control capital flow into a nationally regulated and taxable system. Behind the exorbitant license prices: Simultaneously with the blocking of overseas access, a highly competitive race to issue domestic licenses is underway. Currently, five institutions have passed the first round of qualification review: Techcombank (Technology Commercial Bank), VPBank (Vietnam Hung Van Bank), LPBank (Chain Postal Commercial Bank), VIX Securities (Well-known Securities Firm), and Sun Group (Sun Group, a large conglomerate). As can be seen, this list is primarily composed of traditional financial institutions, with no pure Web3 startups among them. This aligns with the fact that crypto trading in Vietnam is explicitly defined as financial infrastructure, rather than an internet innovation product. The entry threshold for the license is staggeringly high: Minimum paid-in capital: 10 trillion Vietnamese dong (approximately US$400 million). Shareholder structure: Requires joint investment from at least two financial institutions (banks/securities firms/insurance companies/technology companies), with institutional capital accounting for no less than 65%. Foreign investment limit: Foreign shareholding cannot exceed 49%. This is essentially a top-level design aimed at reducing retail and grassroots participation and strengthening financialization, ensuring the market is dominated by well-capitalized, risk-controlled state-owned enterprises and domestic giants. The Impact of the "Siege" Effect This combination of "blocking overseas markets + pilot licensing" will generate complex market effects— First, this policy will undoubtedly benefit the local ecosystem and tax revenue: once users are successfully migrated to local platforms, Vietnam will be able to capture huge transaction fees and levy potential taxes (the personal transaction income tax rate may be 0.1%, and the corporate profit tax rate is 20%). It is estimated that the 0.1% transaction tax alone could bring in approximately $800 million in annual tax revenue. Second, Vietnamese users accustomed to the high liquidity, low fees, and abundant products of global exchanges like Binance may face challenges such as insufficient liquidity, limited product offerings, and a downgraded experience on local platforms in the initial stages. Some users may turn to more covert over-the-counter trading or other DeFi protocols. Furthermore, the deep involvement of banks and securities firms will accelerate the integration of crypto assets with the traditional financial system, potentially giving rise to compliant financial products such as crypto derivatives and collateralized lending based on local platforms. The success of this experiment depends on two key factors: first, whether local licensed platforms can quickly establish competitiveness sufficient to retain users; and second, whether regulators can effectively combat the resulting, more difficult-to-monitor gray-market transactions while blocking overseas channels. Conclusion: Vietnam's actions are not an isolated incident; they reflect a common characteristic of global crypto regulation moving from observation and tacit approval to a stage of sovereignty struggle: countries are all trying to keep the economic value (data, tax revenue, employment) of crypto activities within their own borders. The difference lies in the fact that Vietnam, as an emerging market with highly active retail investors but still developing financial infrastructure, chose the most aggressive and direct method of "traffic interception." This regulatory restructuring not only tests Vietnam's execution capabilities but also provides a model for emerging markets globally. Regardless of the outcome, Vietnam has sounded the alarm for the Southeast Asian crypto market: compliance is no longer an option, but an entry ticket.

data.title: BlackRock vs. Strategy: Who will win the Bitcoin accumulation battle?
data.description:
Author: Jawad Hussain & Translation: Plain Language Blockchain

The world's largest asset management company and a 37-year-old software company that shifted its entire asset holdings to digital assets are locked in an unprecedented race to accumulate Bitcoin in the crypto market.

As of March 16, 2026, BlackRock's iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) held 784,062 Bitcoins. Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) held 761,068 Bitcoins.

The difference between the two is approximately 22,994 coins. At Strategy's current buying pace, this gap could disappear within days.

This is more than just a footnote in the history of digital assets. It is one of the most impactful financial stories of 2026.

Two entities with different structures, motivations, and risk profiles are vying for the same finite asset. Bitcoin has a fixed supply cap of 21 million. For every coin this institution buys, one is no longer waiting to be sold. The race between BlackRock and Strategy is accelerating the supply squeeze long predicted by Bitcoin exchange platforms. BlackRock vs. Strategy: Who Will Win the Bitcoin Accumulation War? This article will analyze how each participant accumulates Bitcoin, what drives their buying speed, what the risks are for both sides, and what the outcome of the pandemic race means for off-exchange investors. Whether you hold IBIT donations, MSTR stock, or directly hold Bitcoin, without exception, the pandemic race directly impacts the markets you participate in. Two Entities, Two Completely Different Models Both BlackRock and Strategy hold massive amounts of Bitcoin. However, their reasons for holding it, the mechanisms by which they do so, and their associated obligations are completely different. How BlackRock Accumulates Bitcoin BlackRock does not buy Bitcoin for itself. The company launched iShares Bitcoin Trust (ticker symbol: IBIT) on Nasdaq in January 2024, providing investors with a regulated tool to gain Bitcoin exposure by directly holding assets. When investors purchase IBIT shares, authorized participants (large financial institutions) buy Bitcoin on the open market and deliver it to the fund. When investors sell IBIT, the process involves: Bitcoin being repurchased from the fund and returned to the market. This means that BlackRock's Bitcoin holdings are a function of investor demand. IBIT holdings grow when institutional and retail buyers want full exposure to Bitcoin through traditional accounts. Holding shrinks when sentiment turns bearish and investors redeem. BlackRock doesn't have a strategic directive to accumulate Bitcoin; it's a custodian. Its Bitcoin holdings belong to IBIT shareholders on economic terms, not BlackRock itself. According to SoSoValue, IBIT has attracted a cumulative net inflow of $63.21 billion since its launch. In the week of March 9-13 alone, IBIT received a total net inflow of $600.1 million, accounting for 78% of the net Bitcoin inflows into ETFs that week. The fund has maintained positive inflows every day since March 9, highlighting the institutional demand driving BlackRock's Bitcoin accumulation. How Strategy Accumulates Bitcoin: Strategy's model is the complete opposite. Instead of waiting for investors to raise funds, the company actively subscribes to funds specifically for purchasing Bitcoin. These funds primarily come from three sources: convertible bonds (debt instruments convertible into MSTR common stock); at-market (ATM) equity offerings (selling new shares directly to the market); and preferred stock instruments, most recently STRC preferred stock with an 11.5% annualized yield, sold to monthly investors who provided direct funding for Bitcoin purchases in exchange for these funds. Once Strategy executes its cash injection, it purchases Bitcoin through institutional trading platforms (primarily Coinbase Prime) and stores the coins in secure cold wallets. The company does not trade these coins or hedge them. There is a simple instruction: buy and hold. This means that Strategy's Bitcoin holdings grow in only one direction. Unlike IBIT, which can be slashed by redemptions, Strategy's Bitcoin inventory grows with each funding round, regardless of market conditions. According to Michael Saylor, in the week leading up to March 2026, Strategy acquired 40,332 Bitcoins, representing 3.0% of the total Bitcoin holdings. As of mid-March 2026, the company had accumulated 88,568 Bitcoins this year, currently holding 3.4%. These figures reflect an accumulation rate never before attempted by a publicly traded company. The current digits: A race could begin in a few days. The current gap is slight since BlackRock briefly surpassed Strategy's holdings in July 2025. As of March 16, 2026, BlackRock held 784,062 Bitcoins, while Strategy held 761,068, a difference of 22,994 Bitcoins. At Strategy's recent rate of purchases of 22,337 contracts per week, the company could almost close the entire gap in a week. At its current rate of purchases of approximately 2,881 contracts per day, it would take about 7 to 8 days to surpass BlackRock's current holdings if IBIT inflows were to completely stop. This last condition is crucial: IBIT is not stagnant in Frankfurt; the fund is absorbing funds daily, meaning that while Strategy is narrowing the gap, the target is constantly moving upwards. The race remained a real hot topic in mid-March because the pace of MSTR purchases coincided perfectly with BlackRock's week-on-week growth. This contraction caused the gap to close faster than most analysts expected. Bitcoin Magazine reported on March 17 that MSTR's stock price was heading towards $150, indicating that market participants were watching the race and betting on Strategy's logic. The core issue isn't just who crosses the holding threshold first, but the impact of these two entities' continued purchases on the available supply in the open market. According to Checkonchain data, as of the end of February 2026, Bitcoin reserves held by national spot ETFs had surged by 1.29 million. Adding Strategy's 761,000, these institutional instruments have absorbed over 2 million Bitcoins. Trading platform inventories are declining. The supply shock driving long-term price increases is not a theoretical future event; it is happening now. The Financial Architecture Behind the Models BlackRock's Structural Advantages BlackRock operates the world's most liquid Bitcoin investment product. According to its own disclosures, IBIT is the Bitcoin trading platform product with the largest trading volume since its inception. The fund manages over $55 billion in Bitcoin assets, providing daily liquidity to investors and charging a 0.25% annual management fee. It is backed by a reputable institution managing over $14 trillion in assets. For institutional investors, IBIT completely eliminates the operational complexities of Bitcoin custody. Bitcoin is held by Coinbase Custody Trust, a qualified custodian regulated under New York banking laws. Investors can access it through their existing accounts, without managing wallets, private keys, or payment processes. This simplicity is invaluable to the funds, sovereign wealth funds, and family offices driving the inflow of IBIT. BlackRock also benefits from structural isolation that Strategy lacks. Because IBIT holdings are linked to investor demand rather than the company's balance sheet, investor panic triggers redemptions, not bankruptcy. BlackRock itself does not face the RMB risk of a Bitcoin price crash. Its IBIT revenue and expenses will decrease, but its financial health remains isolated from the assets it holds. Strategy's Structural Advantages: Strategy's advantage over BlackRock lies in its ability to act while waiting for market approval. IBIT purchases depend on the sentiment of millions of investors, while Strategy can buy whenever it can successfully raise funds. VanEck's research cites Strategy's debt structure as its "silent engine." By early 2026, the firm held a significant amount of zero-interest convertible senior tranches. These instruments allowed Strategy to raise nearly $100 million at zero cost, all of which was used to purchase Bitcoin. The firm also noted the 0.25% annual fee paid by IBIT shareholders, making MSTR a cheap alternative to the costly and expensive ETFs that offer attractive leverage. Strategy's model also benefits from what analysts call the mNAV premium. When a company's market capitalization exceeds the market value of its Bitcoin holdings, the premium allows it to purchase equity at a price that adds to the value of its Bitcoin holdings. This means that the value of Bitcoin added by each newly issued share exceeds its critical threshold. This flywheel can accumulate rapidly when the premium is high and optimism prevails. The company used this dynamic to raise $25.3 billion in 2025, almost entirely by purchasing Bitcoin.

Risks Beared by All Parties

Strategy's Risks

Strategy's risks are real and verifiable.

data.title: Super Micro Co-Founder Arrested in Alleged $2.5B AI Chip Smuggling Scheme to China
data.description:
U.S. authorities have arrested Yih-Shyan Liaw, co-founder of Super Micro Computer, Inc., in connection with an alleged multibillion-dollar scheme to circumvent export controls and funnel advanced artificial intelligence servers to China, marking one of the most significant enforcement actions in the ongoing crackdown on sensitive chip transfers.

🚨BREAKING: SUPER MICRO CO-FOUNDER ARRESTED FOR SMUGGLING $2.5B IN NVIDIA GPUs TO CHINA

>SMCI co-founder Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw arrested today
>personally holds $464 MILLION in SMCI stock
>charged with smuggling BILLIONS in Nvidia servers to china
>used a southeast asian shell… https://t.co/SfIFc0SPedpic.twitter.com/93QE72ddph

— NIK (@ns123abc) March 20, 2026

The U.S. Department of Justice unsealed an indictment charging Liaw, along with sales executives Ruei-Tsang “Steven” Chang and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun, with conspiring to export approximately $2.5 billion worth of servers embedded with restricted graphics processing units to Chinese buyers between 2024 and 2025.

Prosecutors allege the operation relied on falsified documentation, shell entities and staged compliance measures to obscure the true destination of the hardware.

The scale of the activity drew particular scrutiny, with authorities highlighting that more than $510 million in shipments were executed within a two-month window between April and May 2025, underscoring both the speed and coordination of the alleged network.

Concealment network designed to bypass export restrictions

Investigators describe a layered transshipment strategy aimed at evading U.S. export controls on advanced computing hardware. According to the indictment, the defendants allegedly fabricated records, staged non-functional equipment to satisfy audit requirements and routed transactions through intermediary entities to conceal end users.

The servers, built using high-performance components from partners such as Nvidia and Google, were reportedly redirected through third-party jurisdictions before reaching Chinese clients. The use of pass-through companies and falsified inventories allowed the operation to present an appearance of compliance while masking its underlying intent.

这个事情给nvidia 芯片掮客的一记警钟

今天凌晨，Supermicro 的联创 Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw（及2同伙）被抓了。。。（原籍应该是台湾）

事情是因为Wally被指控涉嫌把nvidia的芯片（25亿美金）通过东南亚某国的空壳公司然后转口到中国。… https://t.co/zFaltroZZBpic.twitter.com/wMpvZbPSlN

— danny (@agintender) March 20, 2026

Liaw and Sun have been taken into custody and are expected to appear in the Northern District of California, while Chang, identified as a Taiwanese national, remains at large.

The charges reflect a coordinated effort, according to authorities, to exploit gaps in oversight as demand for AI infrastructure accelerates globally.

Company distances itself as market reaction turns negative

Super Micro Computer, Inc. has not been named as a defendant in the case and has moved to distance itself from the alleged misconduct, stating that the actions described by prosecutors run counter to its internal compliance policies. The company added that it is cooperating fully with the investigation.

Investor sentiment shifted sharply following the announcement. After initially trading higher during the session, Super Micro shares fell more than 13% in after-hours trading, reflecting heightened concern over regulatory exposure and reputational risk.

Super Micro $SMCI is now down almost 15% in after hours following its earnings pic.twitter.com/fz7xibx9lc

— Evan (@StockMKTNewz) April 30, 2024

The case highlights intensifying U.S. scrutiny over the global flow of advanced computing technology, particularly as artificial intelligence infrastructure becomes a strategic asset.

Export controls targeting high-performance chips — many of which are central to data center training and large-scale AI models — have become a focal point in broader geopolitical competition.

By pursuing criminal charges tied to large-scale evasion, authorities are signaling a more aggressive enforcement posture aimed at closing loopholes in complex international supply chains.

*TRUMP'S 10% CHINA TARIFF SET TO TAKE EFFECT AS DEADLINE PASSES

*CHINA TO LEVY TAX ON SOME PRODUCTS IMPORTED FROM US

*CHINA PROBES GOOGLE OVER ALLEGED ANTI-TRUST LAW BREACHES: SAMR

*CHINA SETS EXPORT CONTROL ON TUNGSTEN-RELATED MATERIALS

— Jim Bianco (@biancoresearch) February 4, 2025

At the same time, the case underscores the difficulty of policing cross-border technology flows, where intermediaries and layered transactions can obscure accountability.

As global demand for AI hardware continues to surge, the outcome of the case is likely to shape both compliance expectations and enforcement priorities, with implications extending beyond a single company to the wider semiconductor and data infrastructure ecosystem.

data.title: The sins and non-sinfulness of buying and selling USDT: How can ordinary people use USDT "painlessly"?
data.description:
Since the joint issuance of the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currency" (hereinafter referred to as Notice No. 2.6 or Document No. 42) by eight ministries and commissions in 2026, the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate have held meetings at various levels on multiple occasions, publicly stating that they will increase the investigation and punishment of illegal foreign exchange and money laundering using cryptocurrencies. For example, at a recent important meeting, Supreme People's Court President Zhang Jun clearly pointed out in his work report: "Punish new types of crimes according to law, severely punish crimes such as money laundering and foreign exchange evasion using virtual currencies as a medium, and work together to prevent illegal cross-border fund transfers to safeguard national financial security and social stability." Based on the practical experience of the Sa Jie team, although crypto assets issued on public blockchains generally possess technical characteristics such as peer-to-peer global transmission and anonymous transactions, BTC and ETH, due to their highly volatile value, are positioned more as investment products than value tools. Stablecoins like USDT and USDC, "pegged" to the US dollar, have become a more liquid and widely used payment and settlement tool. Today, the Sa Jie team will use several typical cases to discuss how ordinary people can use USDT "painlessly" in the current situation, solving necessary life and work needs while staying away from red-line risks. I. Crimes Involving Foreign Exchange: Frequent and Prosperous Illegal Business Operations Illegal foreign exchange trading is a highly unique crime in my country's criminal law system. It originates from the only existing single-subject criminal law in my country—the "Decision on Punishing Crimes of Fraudulently Purchasing Foreign Exchange and Evading Foreign Exchange Controls," promulgated by the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress on December 29, 1998. This currently effective single-subject criminal law has withstood decades of changes and twelve amendments to the Criminal Law, remaining in effect to this day, demonstrating the special nature of this crime. In 2019, the Supreme People's Court issued the "Interpretation on Several Issues Concerning the Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Illegally Engaging in Fund Payment and Settlement Business and Illegally Buying and Selling Foreign Exchange," which refined a series of practical standards for the crime of illegal business operations involving the illegal buying and selling of foreign exchange, and clarified the specific amounts for "serious circumstances" and "particularly serious circumstances." Since then, the aforementioned normative document has become the basis for convicting and punishing business activities involving the exchange of crypto assets for fiat currency as illegal business operations. However, it is worth noting that to this day, there is still no clear law, regulation, or normative document defining "using crypto assets to exchange for currency as illegal buying and selling of foreign exchange." In judicial practice, guidance for judgments at the grassroots level is mainly provided through a series of reference cases in the Supreme People's Court's case database, primarily the case of Wan Mouyuan et al. (database entry number: 2025-18-1-085-001). As a result, among crimes involving U.S. currencies, the crime of illegal foreign exchange trading has become the most frequent and prevalent criminal red line risk. II. Using U.S. currencies to assist foreign underground banks in currency exchange and arbitrage is a "crossing the line" behavior and should never be done. Recently, the Supreme People's Court's case database published a reference case: the case of Yan Moukang et al. for illegal business operations (database entry number: 2025-03-1-169-002). In this case, the defendant Yan Moukang met A, a Nigerian who specialized in exchanging Tether (USDT) for RMB, online. A claimed that the fees for buying and selling foreign exchange at banks or foreign exchange companies were high and wanted to exchange Nigerian Naira for RMB through Yan Moukang. The two parties then conspired to use virtual currency as a medium of exchange to circumvent foreign exchange regulations. From September 2020 to April 2021, Yan Moukang, along with defendants Lin Moucheng and Xie Mouwei, quoted A a price that was 1 or 2 cents lower than the daily exchange rate of USDT to RMB offered by virtual currency exchange dealers. After A agreed to the price, A transferred USDT purchased with his local currency, Naira, to a virtual currency exchange account controlled by Yan Moukang and others. Yan Moukang and others then sold the USDT to convert it into RMB (withdrawal), and, according to A's instructions, paid the RMB to a designated bank account in China. From September to December 2020, Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng jointly received 3,275,396.3 USDT from A Mou and sold them to domestic currency dealers, totaling RMB 21,293,872.33, with a profit of at least RMB 32,753.96. From January to April 2021, Yan Moukang, Lin Moucheng, and Xie Mouwei jointly received 1,290,342.1 USDT from A Mou and sold them to domestic currency dealers, totaling RMB 8,380,182.78, with a profit of at least RMB 12,903.42. Ultimately, although Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng's profits were relatively low and they had already returned the stolen goods before the judgment, they were still sentenced to five years in prison for illegal business operations. This case is a typical example of using personal encrypted asset accounts to help overseas underground banks bypass my country's foreign exchange controls and carry out illegal currency exchange. Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng were clearly aware that A was engaged in foreign exchange exchange business. Their main purpose in helping A withdraw U, which was exchanged using foreign legal tender, was very clear: to bypass foreign exchange regulatory authorities and avoid paying relevant handling fees. From this case, it is clear that Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng's actions were extremely counterproductive. In a seven-month-long "arbitrage" scheme, they only profited a little over 40,000 yuan, a profit that is probably less than that of the breakfast shop selling pancakes downstairs, yet they paid the heavy price of five years in prison.

III. Cryptocurrency dealers fabricating transactions to conceal foreign exchange transactions is a "crossing the line" behavior and should never be tolerated

The case of Wan Mouyuan et al.'s illegal business operations mentioned above (entry number: 2025-18-1-085-001) is a well-known typical case of using virtual currency as a medium to buy and sell foreign exchange, constituting the crime of illegal business operations. In judicial practice, there is no doubt that such cryptocurrency dealers who use U as a medium for exchanging US dollars and RMB to evade foreign exchange supervision should be prosecuted for the crime of illegal business operations. However, the Sa Jie team needs to solemnly remind our partners that such behavior is often accompanied by false trade, and such behavior will breed more criminal risks.

Taking the case of Wan Mouyuan and others' illegal business operations as an example, after the transaction was agreed upon or the exchanged US dollars were received, the customers would typically transfer the RMB used to purchase the US dollars to a bank account provided by Wan Mouyuan (in this case, there was a mix of legal tender and US dollars). At this point, Wan Mouyuan would usually collect the RMB payment from the customers through fictitious transactions, using the method of payment for goods, thus completing the transaction. During this process, in order to comply with the bank's foreign exchange settlement requirements, Wan Mouyuan forged a large number of false transaction materials. Even to achieve the requirement of consistency between the contract, funds, and invoices, he issued VAT invoices to customers through entities he registered or controlled. Therefore, Wan Mouyuan's act of fabricating transactions to conceal foreign exchange transactions actually constitutes not only the crime of illegal business operations but also the crime of issuing false VAT invoices (dealt with in another case), and he is very likely to be punished for multiple crimes. IV. How can ordinary people use U "painlessly"? Avoiding "business activities" is key. In practice, Chinese judicial organs generally focus on the following situations to determine whether a party's behavior constitutes "business activity." (I) Whether there is a profit-making purpose. Profit-making purpose is the essential characteristic of business activities. Generally, as long as the actor subjectively intends to obtain economic benefits through transactions, regardless of whether actual profit is made, as long as there is an active pursuit of profit or indirect avoidance of loss, it can be determined that there is a profit-making purpose. For example, in the illegal business cases of Yan Moukang et al. and Wan Mouyuan et al., the defendants all "took a cut" in the relevant transactions or earned a certain price difference by adjusting the transaction price and thus profited. Actions not intended for profit, such as individuals using USDT to purchase consumer goods, charitable acts (such as non-profit organizations providing free education or charitable services), or acts solely intended for illegal possession (such as fraud or theft), do not constitute business activities. (II) Whether there is market transaction activity. The so-called "market transaction" activity mainly depends on whether the relevant transaction occurs between equal market entities, whether it involves the exchange of goods or the provision of services, whether there is business promotion to unspecified or potential trading partners, and whether the trading partners (goods/services) have open market liquidity (it cannot be a specific, closed transaction between individuals). (III) Whether it possesses continuity and repetition. Continuity and repetition are important characteristics of business activities. The actor does not conduct transactions accidentally or in a single instance, but engages in similar business activities in a continuous and repetitive manner. For example, in the cases of illegal business operations by Yan Moukang et al. and Wan Mouyuan et al., the parties completed dozens or even hundreds of foreign exchange transactions within a few months, demonstrating obvious continuity and repetition. In conclusion, under the current high-pressure regulatory and judicial crackdown, USDT is no longer simply a "digital token," but a core risk point in cross-border capital and foreign exchange regulation. For ordinary people, the safest way to use USDT "painlessly" is only one: stick to personal use, stay away from commercial transactions, refuse intermediaries, and avoid currency exchange.

data.title: A hairdryer uncovered the world's largest Nvidia chip smuggling case.
data.description:
Author: David｜Deep Tide TechFlow

Early this morning, Wally Liaw, co-founder of Supermicro, was arrested in California.

Supermicro is one of the world's largest AI server manufacturers and a core supply chain partner of Nvidia. Last year, it secured $13 billion in orders for Nvidia's Blackwell chips in just one quarter.

Liaw has been with the company since its founding in 1993 and is currently its Senior Vice President and a member of the Board of Directors.

He is charged with smuggling $2.5 billion worth of Nvidia AI chip servers into China through shell companies in Southeast Asia. The maximum sentence is 20 years in prison. The U.S. Department of Justice has characterized this case as the largest AI chip smuggling case in history. However, the details of the case are very much like a scene from a movie. According to the indictment, Liaw and his accomplices arranged for a Southeast Asian company to act as a "front," purchasing large quantities of servers equipped with Nvidia GPUs from Supermicro under the guise of personal use. After the servers arrived in Southeast Asia, staff removed the original packaging, packed them into unmarked boxes, and shipped them to China. The real servers were shipped away, but Supermicro's compliance team and the U.S. Department of Commerce regularly inspected the warehouse and scanned serial numbers; the warehouse couldn't be empty. So they manufactured tens of thousands of fake servers. The outer casings were identical to the real ones, but they contained no chips and couldn't be powered on; they were specifically displayed in the warehouse for auditors to see. Having fake servers wasn't enough; the serial numbers on the packaging also had to match. Workers used a hairdryer to heat the serial number sticker on the genuine product's packaging, peeled it off completely, and then stuck it onto the box of the fake server... Auditors scanned the code, and the system showed everything was normal. The factory's surveillance cameras captured that scene of the hairdryer being used. But the genuine product was already on its way to China. Even more outrageous, on one occasion, when Supermicro's own compliance team came to investigate, Sun, an accomplice, took photos and videos of fake servers and sent them to the auditors to prove that "the goods were all there." On another occasion, export control officials from the U.S. Department of Commerce personally came to inspect. Someone on site directly impersonated a lawyer to receive them... The main culprit, Liaw, is 71 years old and holds $430 million worth of Supermicro stock. Logically, he shouldn't be short of money. There's another detail in the indictment: a broker forwarded Liaw a news link about several Chinese people being arrested in the U.S. for smuggling AI chips. Liaw's reply was a string of crying face emojis, and then he continued. Following the announcement, Supermicro's stock fell 13% in after-hours trading. The company stated that Liaw had been suspended, Sun, the person involved, had been dismissed, and Steven Chang, the sales manager of the Taiwan office, was currently at large. Supermicro was not listed as a defendant. The company stated that it has a "comprehensive compliance system." However, this is not the first time this company's compliance system has run into problems. $2.5 Billion: A Chip Smuggling Production Line This smuggling chain wasn't built overnight. The indictment shows that Liaw was very proactive in orchestrating the operation. He directly used encrypted communication tools to ask the heads of Southeast Asian companies: How much can you eat in January? February? March? April? Just give a rough estimate, and then we'll use that number to apply for chip quotas from Nvidia. So the process was: First, confirm the demand from Chinese buyers, then use that quantity to negotiate quotas with Nvidia, arguing that the Southeast Asian companies were "for their own use." Nvidia approved the quota, the servers were assembled in the US, shipped to Taiwan, and then handed over to a Southeast Asian company. The Southeast Asian company unpacked them and shipped them to China. From customers to suppliers to logistics, Liaw single-handedly streamlined the entire chain. He wasn't a middleman; he was the co-founder and head of business development at Supermicro. Using his own company's supply chain, he smuggled his own company's products. Between 2024 and 2025, servers worth $2.5 billion flowed out through this channel. The indictment states that this scale gradually expanded, using the phrase "becoming increasingly audacious." The most audacious transaction occurred between late April and mid-May 2025. In three weeks, $510 million worth of servers were transshipped from the United States to China via Southeast Asia. Why the sudden acceleration? Possibly because Liaw saw a White House statement. In early 2025, the US government announced a new export control rule for AI products, expected to take effect in mid-May, further tightening the review of chip exports to Southeast Asia and other regions. Liaw forwarded this message to the head of the Southeast Asian company, adding the comment: "We need to speed these up before May 13!" May 13th is the effective date of the new regulations. Before then, existing vulnerabilities are still usable. So the last three weeks became a race against time. What normally takes months to complete was compressed into twenty days. Meanwhile, Steven Chang in the Taiwan office was responsible for another task: ensuring no one actually audited. The indictment states that he prevented auditors from inspecting the actual storage areas of the servers in the Southeast Asian data center and specifically assigned what he called a "friendly" auditor to conduct the review. All the forged documents, fake servers, and labels on the hairdryers were designed to prevent the production line from being disrupted. The production line reached its peak speed in the last three weeks. But Supermicro wasn't the only one involved in chip smuggling. Over the past two years, the U.S. Department of Justice has dismantled several chip smuggling networks. These networks ranged in size from tens of millions to hundreds of millions of dollars, with increasingly outrageous methods. The identities of the participants also evolved. The stricter the chip ban, the more respectable the smugglers become. Supermicro wasn't the first to be caught. Looking back two years, the chip smuggling business has undergone three generations of evolution. The identities of the participants have upgraded, the methods have become more and more like legitimate business, and the scale has grown larger and larger. The first generation was "ant-like" smuggling. When the chip ban was first introduced in 2022, smuggling was still physically demanding. International students, tourists, and personal shoppers would carry one or two Nvidia GPUs at a time, hiding them in their personal luggage to cross customs, claiming they were for personal use if stopped. Some people stuffed chips into boxes containing live lobsters, while others strapped them to their bodies and concealed them with fake pregnant bellies. Once in Shenzhen's Huaqiangbei, these chips have a complete after-sales industry chain. There are specialized workshops that repair faulty and disassembled chips, processing over 500 chips per month, charging $1400 to $2800 per chip. After repair, they are sold to data centers and AI startups. At this stage, smugglers have no company, no documents; they rely on human flesh and audacity. The amount involved in each transaction is small, but every little bit helps.

The second generation started using companies, engaging in deceptive practices.

In late 2025, the U.S. Department of Justice dismantled a smuggling network codenamed "Operation Gatekeeper." The mastermind was a 43-year-old man named Alan Hsu, living in Houston. He used a company under his name to purchase Nvidia chips in bulk from Lenovo and ship them to a warehouse in New Jersey. The company, Janford Realtor, is registered as a real estate company. It has never conducted a single real estate transaction. After the chips arrived at the warehouse, workers removed the original Nvidia labels and replaced them with the name of a fictitious company—"SANDKYAN." They were then transshipped through Malaysia and Thailand before finally entering China. The entire network shipped over $160 million worth of chips in eight months. The most dramatic moment in the case occurred during the raid. FBI agents infiltrated the New Jersey warehouse beforehand and secretly removed all the GPUs. The smugglers, discovering the goods were missing, assumed they had been stolen and sent people to the warehouse to "redeem" them. The ransom was negotiated at $1 million, and the person who came to make the contact was caught on the spot by the FBI. The third generation is like Supermicro, a company with specialized expertise. No longer a fake company, no longer a middleman, it's personally managed by the co-founder of a listed company. No need to buy chips from others; their own company is Nvidia's core supplier. No need to forge buyer identities; they use their own supply chain, their own customer relationships, and their own quotas to apply for chips, and then bypass their own compliance team internally. From small-scale operations to fake real estate companies, and then to executives of Silicon Valley listed companies. All three generations of smugglers share one thing in common: The end buyers are all in China, and the chips are all from Nvidia. The difference lies in the fact that those who crossed customs with backpacks in 2022 were gambling with their freedom. Those who are operating from Silicon Valley offices in 2026 are gambling with a company worth tens of billions of dollars. With each escalation of the ban, the profits from smuggling jump, and the social standing of those willing to participate also rises. So, just how profitable is this business? The Black Market Price of a Chip Why can't chip smuggling be stopped? The answer lies in the price. According to multiple overseas media reports, Nvidia's high-end GPUs are priced about 50% higher on the Chinese black market than through official channels in the US. The ban created a price difference, the price difference created profits, and profits created smugglers. Demand is inelastic. Ray Wang, an analyst at semiconductor analysis firm SemiAnalysis, told CNBC: More than 60% of China's leading AI models are still running on Nvidia hardware. Training a large model requires tens of thousands of high-end GPUs; without even one, the entire process cannot begin. This means that Chinese AI companies don't just "want to buy" Nvidia chips; they "have to buy them." [Image source: GamersNexus In-depth Investigation of Huaqiangbei Chip Market] What about the supply side? After chips leave Nvidia's factory, they pass through distributors, system integrators, and server manufacturers, each a potential entry point for smuggling. US export controls primarily restrict access at the sales and shipping stage, relying on buyers to declare their end-users. Once the chips leave the US, subsequent stages largely depend on self-regulation. A Financial Times report last year estimated that in the three months following April 2025 alone, over $1 billion worth of embargoed chips would flow into China through various channels. Industry insiders estimate the entire black market's monthly turnover could be in the billions of dollars... Ironically, the US government's own policies are also wavering. Last December, the Department of Justice announced with great fanfare that it had dismantled the Operation Gatekeeper smuggling network and arrested several suspects. However, on the same day, Trump announced on Truth Social that Nvidia would be allowed to sell H200 chips to China. Later, the US government opened another loophole, allowing Nvidia to sell lower-performance H20 chips to China, on the condition that Nvidia pay 15% of its sales revenue to the US government. This simultaneously cracking down on smuggling, selling the chips themselves, and taking a cut makes the entire ban system very difficult to explain. If you tell a chip broker that smuggling is illegal, he might reply: "The government itself sells them too, they just call it 'conditional export.'" The deeper paradox lies in the fact that Nvidia is currently the world's most valuable company, with a market capitalization of $4.3 trillion. The more advanced its chips, the greater the demand, the higher the black market premium, and the more lucrative the smuggling profits. A hairdryer can't blow away the demand. The government itself is cracking down on smuggling while simultaneously approving exports. What signal do people within the chip industry's upstream and downstream companies see? The protagonist of this article, Supermicro, also helped Musk build the Colossus AI computing cluster in 122 days and secured a $13 billion order from Nvidia's Blackwell last quarter. The people who built the servers and those who smuggled them were the same group. A $2.5 billion smuggling case ultimately came to light because of a hair dryer. Surveillance footage showed workers using hair dryers to tear off labels, and it's now on the US Department of Justice's website, visible to anyone. In the same week that Liaw was arrested, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang publicly stated that the company was about to face a $1 trillion backlog of chip orders. A trillion dollars in demand is there, and so is the black market premium. Perhaps the next person willing to pick up a hairdryer won't be far off.

data.title: Crypto fanatics in prison and the 30 billion illusion of the AI ​​era
data.description:
That crypto scammer who misappropriated massive amounts of client funds almost became the most successful investor in the AI ​​era.

Imagine the time is April 2022.

ChatGPT hadn't even been born yet, and the Cambrian explosion of AI was still more than half a year away. Throughout Silicon Valley, there were still more voices talking about the metaverse and Web3 than about grand models.

An AI security startup called Anthropic is currently raising Series B funding.

At that moment, a man approached and handed over a check. Face value: $500 million.
