He swallowed 86% of this funding round, raising the valuation of a company with zero annual revenue and a bleak future to nearly $6 billion. In the prehistoric winter of AI, this was nothing short of a crazy gamble. The person who handed over the check was Sam Bankman-Fried, the man known as SBF with the afro. At that time, he was the emperor of the cryptocurrency exchange FTX, basking in the glory of being the king of cryptocurrencies. The person who accepted the check was Dario Amodei, co-founder of Anthropic. Today, we know the outcome of two things: First, the $500 million was deposits from FTX users, and SBF was imprisoned for 25 years for fraud. Second, Anthropic has become one of the most important AI companies. In February 2026, it completed a $30 billion Series G funding round at a valuation of $380 billion. Amazon, Google, Nvidia, Microsoft… giants lined up to invest in it. If that money were clean, and if SBF hadn't gone to jail, the approximately 8% stake he acquired for $500 million would be worth over $30 billion today. A 60-fold return—a figure worthy of legendary status in venture capital history. A question naturally arises: how did a crypto gambler manage to find the most dazzling jewel in the crown of AI before everyone else could even see the way forward? The answer is: He didn't find it by chance; he was brought to the door by the "circle." You might not know that the SBF and Anthropic teams belong to the same niche elite circle—Effective Altruism (EA). This circle doesn't value personal relationships; it only focuses on creating long-term value with maximum efficiency. SBF believes in radical altruism that involves making money and donating it, while Anthropic is dedicated to creating safe and controllable AI. Their underlying logic is completely aligned. At that time, Anthropic lacked funds and resources, but it possessed the core team that had left OpenAI and was committed to long-term research and development in AI security. SBF, on the other hand, had money and influence and urgently needed to invest in core technologies to shed the speculative label of crypto capital and solidify its elite image. Beyond the resonance within specific circles, this significant investment was also a well-thought-out strategic move to secure a foothold in the ecosystem. In SBF's business blueprint, crypto was never the end goal. He had long recognized that simply speculating on cryptocurrencies and operating exchanges was merely a zero-sum game. The power to define the next generation of the internet would undoubtedly lie in the hands of those who integrate AI and Web3. AI is the engine of productivity, and Web3 is the underlying layer of value distribution; only by binding the two together can a closed-loop ecosystem be built. In 2022, the AI ​​sector was still in the darkness before dawn, and traditional capital dared not invest heavily. SBF seized the opportunity, investing $500 million to lock in leading companies in the AI ​​security sector, preemptively securing a core position in the future digital ecosystem. True capital investment is about betting on the sector ten years from now, not on immediate profits. Even at the peak of a bull market, SBF is well aware of the crypto industry's fatal flaws: regulatory oversight is looming, the bubble is thick, and it's highly cyclical, making a collapse imminent. Money earned through exchanges and hedge funds is ultimately wealth floating on a bubble, unable to withstand any storm. Investing in Anthropic is like buying a hedging policy for crypto assets. AI is a globally recognized core technology, with favorable policies and strong growth certainty. Investing quick crypto profits in long-term technology sectors can both diversify the systemic risks of a single industry and allow one's capital portfolio to expand beyond the narrow confines of the crypto sphere. Top players understand the importance of being prepared for unforeseen circumstances and use cross-industry investments to mitigate bubble risks. **Irresistible Dirty Money** But Dario Amodei wasn't a fool. He saw enough warning signs from SBF, so he set up a firewall: SBF received shares without voting rights, completely isolating it from the board and corporate governance. So why, knowing the danger, did he still swallow this drastic measure? In the AI ​​startup wasteland of early 2022, a believer who could write a check for $500 million at once was an irresistible lifeline. This money was the hard currency for purchasing exorbitantly priced GPUs and assembling a top-tier team. SBF's entire narrative—rapidly accumulating wealth in the gray areas of crypto to fund the grand philanthropy of secure AI—is an extreme example of this logic. Before the crimes were exposed, it appeared to be a radical yet legitimate philosophical choice. Anthropic's acceptance of the money, in a sense, was tacit approval of this logic. They used governance firewalls to isolate the risks of the methods, but took the fuel for the consequences. In business history, many great beginnings have been accompanied by controversies surrounding the original sin of their initial capital. But this time, the controversy came too quickly and too violently. Seven months later, the FTX empire collapsed within days, and SBF's methods were proven to be outright fraud. That $500 million investment was frozen as embezzled assets. During the lengthy bankruptcy liquidation, this portion of equity was divided and auctioned off, ultimately recovering $1.34 billion for FTX's creditors. $1.34 billion, a figure that brought some comfort to the creditors. But what if the liquidation team had waited another two years? What if they hadn't been in a hurry to cash out, and had held on until Anthropic completed its $380 billion Series G funding round? That figure would have exceeded $30 billion. Between $1.34 billion and $30 billion lies a $28.66 billion opportunity cost difference. It became the largest wisp of illusory smoke on the ruins of FTX, a parallel universe dream that could have existed. This adds a layer of absurd tragedy to the fraud, taking on a financial dimension. Even more ironically, one of the buyers who participated in the auction and acquired these shares was Jane Street, SBF's former employer. History, in this way, completed a cruel cycle. A check folds into two universes. The two protagonists of the story went to opposite ends of the earth. SBF, in prison, calculates his earliest release date in 2049. The very means he used to practice his philosophy ultimately consumed him. Meanwhile, Anthropic, with that controversial initial funding, coupled with countless clean and massive investments afterward, has positioned itself as one of the rule-makers of the AI ​​era. It stands on the stage negotiating with the Pentagon, attempting to draw a line for the safety of AI. They both originated from the same intellectual soil: a rational fanaticism that willingly tolerated unconventional methods to cope with extreme uncertainty and grand goals. SBF turned unconventional methods into crimes, while Anthropic, within the red lines, built a $380 billion empire with its grand goals. Faith can drive cutting-edge creation, but it can also package the most greedy plunder. This is perhaps the coldest revelation this $500 million ill-gotten investment leaves for business history: they sometimes share the same discourse, and even share the same check in their early stages. The difference lies not in the starting point, but in the unwavering reverence for that insurmountable bottom line. That check folded into two worlds.
